data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "An abrupt shift to a colder, more variable climate around 1195‑1155 BCE caused recurrent early‑season frosts that destroyed cereal yields across the Eastern Mediterranean.","mechanism": "Climate‑induced agricultural shortfall reduced state tax revenues, precipitating elite indebtedness and forced abandonment of peripheral settlements.","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece","Hittite Anatolia","Ugarit","Levantine city‑states"],"excludes": ["Egypt"]},"forbidden": [{"case": "Archaeobotanical records reveal no evidence of early‑season frost layers or sudden temperature drop in core sites", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}],"prose_notes": "The hypothesis emphasizes a rapid climatic pivot rather than a protracted drought, distinguishing it from prevailing megadrought models."}
